context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: "Moral Moment" Vignette

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Session 3
Weight: 5% of the final grade

Assignment:
Think of an occasion of moral deliberation or resolve in which you were directly involved as a participant (you don't have to be the main actor, but you must have something at stake in the situation). This might be a moment of confusion when a quandary about what it means to be a good person presents itself, or a moment of clarity when a course of action is determined. It might be an occasion of deliberation, when people debate the meaning of a shared value, or weigh between competing values. Or it might be a solitary moment of quiet conviction. It could be a moment of moral triumph, of achieving a goal of self-realization, or a moment of failure, of falling short of one's own expectations. This could be a major, life-changing event (like a religious conversion experience) or it could be a small, everyday occurrence (like choosing whether or not to give money to a panhandler), as long as it is personally meaningful in some way.

In a few paragraphs, describe the situation in sufficient detail so that your understanding of the participants' moral viewpoints and reasons for (or doubts about) acting one way or another are clear. This should be a real situation, in the near or distant past, not an abstract ethical deliberation (like debates over when self-driving cars should be allowed to kill pedestrians).

Format:
- Write out this brief vignette in 2–3 pages (double spaced).
- We will come back to this initial piece of writing later in the semester, continuing to build upon it as the basis for further discussion, reflection, and analysis.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help picking a good real-life moral moment for the vignette rn. i have a couple ideas but idk which one fits the assignment better.
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's figure that out together! What are the ideas you're considering? Share them and we can think through how well each one fits the assignment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them choose between their ideas. Rather than picking for them, I should ask them to share their ideas and then guide them to evaluate the ideas against the assignment criteria themselves. This uses the Socratic method and guided discovery while keeping the student in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: i'm thinking maybe one about whether to report a classmate for cheating, and another about lying to my parents about where i was. do those sound too basic or are they okay?
exchanges.tutor: Before I weigh in, let's have you do the evaluating. Look back at the assignment description — what does it say makes a situation a good fit for this vignette? Does either of your ideas check those boxes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their ideas for them, which I should redirect. Instead, I should ask them to look at the assignment criteria themselves and assess their own ideas against those criteria. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method without giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: i think it needs to be real, personally meaningful, and have actual moral conflict instead of just a random story. both kinda fit, but the cheating one feels easier to show different people's reasons maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking — you've identified the right criteria. Now, what does "having something at stake" mean to you personally in each situation? That might help you figure out which one gives you more to work with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a good job identifying the key criteria and is starting to self-evaluate. I should affirm their reasoning process and push them to dig a little deeper into what makes one situation richer than the other for THIS specific assignment, without choosing for them.

exchanges.student: for the cheating one, i'd risk losing a friendship or getting dragged into it, so that feels higher-stakes. the parents one matters too, but rn it feels more like i just know i was wrong lol.
exchanges.tutor: That's an interesting distinction. The assignment asks you to make the participants' moral viewpoints and reasons clear — not just your own. In the parents situation, who else has a stake and a perspective? And in the cheating one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress in self-evaluating. They've identified that the cheating scenario has more complexity and stakes. I should push them to think about the assignment's emphasis on moral viewpoints and reasons — not just stakes — to help them see which situation has more analytical depth to write about.

exchanges.student: yeah, i see it. in the cheating one there's me, the classmate, maybe other students, and maybe the professor too, so it seems richer tbh.
exchanges.tutor: You've worked that out well. So now that you have a situation in mind, what specific details would you need to include to make those different moral viewpoints come across clearly to a reader?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially reasoned their way to a good choice on their own. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and then push them forward toward actually starting to write, since the assignment requires a concrete, detailed description.

exchanges.student: i guess i need the context, what exactly i knew, what my friend said, what i was afraid would happen, and why reporting or not reporting each seemed right/wrong. i'm not fully sure how much detail is too much tho.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually gives you a clue about that — it says 'in sufficient detail so that your understanding of the participants' moral viewpoints and reasons for acting one way or another are clear.' So what's your benchmark for 'sufficient' based on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid sense of what to include. Their concern about 'too much detail' is worth addressing briefly by pointing them back to the assignment's own language about sufficient detail. I should redirect them to the assignment itself rather than answering for them.

exchanges.student: maybe enough detail that someone can see why each option felt morally possible, not just what happened. but ngl i still need help turning that into an intro.
exchanges.tutor: Writing the intro is your job — I won't do that part for you! But here's a nudge: where in the situation does the moral tension first appear for you? That might be a natural place to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to help them write, which I must refuse per the rules. I should clearly but kindly set that boundary and redirect them toward starting on their own, perhaps by asking a question that helps them think about how to open the vignette.

exchanges.student: ok, i think it starts when my classmate asked me not to say anything after i saw what happened. can u help me check if that's a strong opening point?
exchanges.tutor: You tell me — does starting there immediately put the moral tension and the stakes on the table for a reader? What would someone understand about the situation from that opening moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to evaluate their opening point, which risks me doing the work for them. Instead, I should ask them to evaluate it against the assignment criteria themselves, using the Socratic method to guide them toward their own judgment.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think so, bc right away it shows loyalty vs fairness and that i had to decide something. i think that's prob the best place to start.
exchanges.tutor: You've done solid prep work here. You know your situation, your key details, and your opening point. Time to stop planning and start writing — get a first draft down and you can always refine from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through this well and reached a confident conclusion on their own. We've covered enough ground — situation chosen, key details identified, opening point established. Per the rules about redundancy and moving forward, I should affirm their progress and push them to actually start drafting rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, that's fair lol. i'll draft it from that moment first, then see if the viewpoints are clear.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. Remember, this vignette will come back later in the semester as a foundation for further work, so it's worth getting those moral viewpoints genuinely clear. Come back with a draft when you're ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and is ready to write. This is a good closing point for our exchange. I should briefly affirm their approach and encourage them to come back once they have a draft, since the assignment notes this vignette will be built upon later.
